front: Poem: Death 'kindly stopped for me' in a carriage; they 'passed the Fields of Gazing Grain' and the speaker 'surmised the Horses' Heads / Were toward Eternity'
back: Because I could not stop for Death (Emily Dickinson)
work: Because I could not stop for Death

front: Poem: ends 'I could not see to see' after a fly's 'Blue - uncertain - stumbling Buzz'; 'the Stillness in the Room / Was like the Stillness in the Air'
back: I heard a Fly buzz - when I died (Emily Dickinson)
work: I heard a Fly buzz - when I died

front: Poem: 'Mourners, to and fro' tread inside the speaker's brain; 'a Plank in Reason, broke' and the speaker drops 'down, and down'
back: I felt a Funeral, in my Brain (Emily Dickinson)
work: I felt a Funeral, in my Brain

front: Poem: winter light with the 'Heft of Cathedral Tunes' inflicts a 'Heavenly Hurt' and an 'imperial affliction'
back: There's a certain Slant of light (Emily Dickinson)
work: There's a certain Slant of light

front: Poem: hope as a bird that 'perches in the soul' and 'sings the tune without the words,' heard 'in the chillest land'
back: Hope is the thing with feathers (Emily Dickinson)
work: Hope is the thing with feathers

front: Poem: the speaker is a loaded gun; 'Mountains straight reply' when fired; has 'the power to kill, / Without - the power to die'; smile produces a 'Vesuvian face'
back: My Life had stood - a Loaded Gun (Emily Dickinson)
work: My Life had stood - a Loaded Gun

front: Poem: 'Rowing in Eden!'; 'Done with the Compass - / Done with the Chart!'; ending controversially with 'Might I but moor - tonight - / In Thee!'
Poem: a snake riddle poem; speaker is left 'Zero at the Bone'; newspaper The Republican altered its punctuation
back: A narrow Fellow in the Grass (Emily Dickinson)
work: A narrow Fellow in the Grass

front: Work: three passionate letters to a mysterious 'Master' in which the author calls herself 'Daisy'; one opens 'If you saw a bullet hit a Bird'
Author: corresponded with Thomas Wentworth Higginson after his Atlantic Monthly essay; asked 'are you too deeply occupied to say if my Verse is alive?'
Author: poems first published posthumously by Mabel Loomis Todd, who standardized dashes and capitalization; Thomas Johnson's 1955 edition restored originals
Poem: 'Then there's a pair of us! / Don't tell!'; asks 'How dreary - to be - Somebody!' imagining 'an admiring Bog'
back: I'm Nobody! Who are you? (Emily Dickinson)
